title: Affine Subcode Ensemble Decoding for Degeneracy-Aware Quantum Error Correction
authors: Leo Wursthorn, Jonathan Mandelbaum, Sisi Miao, Hedongliang Liu, Holger Jäkel, Stergios Koutsioumpas, Laurent Schmalen
abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check codes are promising candidates for low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computing, but degeneracy is known to impair the convergence of belief-propagation (BP) decoding of these codes. In this work, we show that appending linearly independent rows to a check matrix of a stabilizer code can reduce the search space for a valid degenerate solution. Motivated by this, we extend the recently proposed affine subcode ensemble decoding technique from the classical to the quantum setting. Moreover, we employ overcomplete matrices for each decoding path. Monte-Carlo simulations on toric and generalized bicycle codes demonstrate improved convergence and reduced logical error rate.
summary: This paper introduces a new decoding technique called Affine Subcode Ensemble Decoding (aSCED) designed to handle the degeneracy issues in QLDPC codes. By using additional parity checks to split large degeneracy sets, the method improves the convergence of Belief Propagation and achieves lower logical error rates than existing decoders.
main_problem: The inherent degeneracy in Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) codes causes Belief Propagation (BP) decoders to oscillate or fail to converge, leading to high logical error rates.
main_result: The proposed aSCED framework significantly improves decoding convergence and reduces logical error rates, outperforming standard MWPM and BP-OSD decoders on Toric and Generalized Bicycle codes.
method: The authors extend the classical Affine Subcode Ensemble Decoding (aSCED) to the quantum setting by appending linearly independent 'splitter' rows to the check matrix and using an ensemble of parallel decoding paths with overcomplete matrices.
model_or_system: The study focuses on stabilizer codes, specifically the Toric code and Generalized Bicycle (GB) codes, under a depolarizing error channel where physical qubits are subject to independent Pauli X, Y, and Z errors.
key_observables: Logical Error Rate (LER), Type I failure fraction (non-convergence), and Type II failure fraction (logical errors).
important_parameters: Physical error probability (p), ensemble size (K), number of batches (L), and number of splitters (delta).
main_assumptions: The error model assumes a depolarizing channel with independent errors per qubit and relies on the stabilizer formalism.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the aSCED batch block diagram; Figure 2 compares LER for various aSCED configurations; Figure 3 compares aSCED to MWPM decoders on the Toric code; Figure 4 shows the reduction in Type I failures; Figure 5 compares aSCED to BP-OSD on GB codes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of degeneracy-induced decoding failure, defines the mathematical framework of degeneracy sets, proposes the aSCED method using splitters and overcomplete matrices, presents Monte-Carlo simulation results on Toric and GB codes, and concludes with a comparison against state-of-the-art decoders.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Block diagram of an aSCED batch with 2∆distinct paths decoding with the same extended check matrix H(ℓ). Each path uses a different syndrome configuration gδ ∈F∆ 2 , δ ∈[2∆], corresponding to the ∆splitters.
Fig. 2. LER of different {BP4, OBP4}-aSCEDK configurations on the J46, 2, 9K GB code with stand-alone BP4 (or OBP4) as a baseline.

Fig. 2 shows the LER of different {BP4, OBP4}-aSCEDK configurations on the J46, 2, 9K GB code. All configurations outperform their respective stand-alone BP4 decoder (baseline) across the entire simulated regime. Furthermore, we observe that, when increasing the ensemble size K, increasing L yields a larger performance gain than increasing ∆, i.e., increasing the number of batches is prefer- able over increasing the number of paths per batch. For in- stance, for BP4, consider the gap between K =4, ∆=2, L=1 and K =16, ∆=4, L=1, compared to the the gap between K =4, ∆=2, L=1 and K =16, ∆=2, L=4, where the latter is significantly larger. This behavior may be attributed to increased diversity...
Fig. 5. LER of OBP4-aSCEDK, K ∈{16, 64, 256}, and BP-OSD on the J126, 28, 8K GB code. BP and OBP4 are limited to Imax =200 decoding iterations. OSD post-processing is applied at order 10.
Fig. 3. LER of OBP4-aSCEDK, K ∈{64, 256}, and state-of-the-art de- coders, namely MWPM and correlated MWPM, on the J128, 2, 8K toric code.
